text: After establishing pneumoperitoneum, four robotic trochars and two laparoscopic trochars are inserted for upper abdominal surgery. The surgeon dissects adhesions between the omentum and gallbladder, visualizes the large choledochal cyst, and incises the peritoneum between the duodenum and cyst, mobilizing the duodenum from the cyst wall. Attention is then turned to the left side of the hepatic hilum to dissect the proper hepatic artery and its branches, removing lymphatic tissues from the artery.

The surgeon visualizes the pulsatile proper hepatic artery running along the left side of the choledochal cyst. The choledochal cyst is encircled with umbilical tape, allowing retraction superiorly for dissection of the inferior pole within the pancreatic head. The cyst wall is circumferentially dissected from the pancreatic tissues, with the abrupt tapering marking the inferior edge, which is ligated and transected.

The choledochal cyst is retracted superiorly to dissect the posterior wall from the hepatic hilum. The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy using a fundus-first approach, transecting the cystic artery. The cystic duct remains attached to the choledochal cyst. The bile duct bifurcation is visible, with the right hepatic artery preserved. The superior edge of the cyst is clearly visualized, and the normal caliber common hepatic duct is transected with an endoscopic stapler.

The surgeon creates a Roux limb measuring approximately forty centimeters by transecting the jejunum with an endo-GIA stapler. A jejunal jejunostomy is formed using another firing of the stapler, and the enterotomy is closed with sutures. The stapled stump of the common hepatic duct is resected, and an enterotomy is made in the Roux limb.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy using interrupted fibro-PDS sutures. The posterior row of sutures is placed first, followed by the anterior row. The completed anastomosis is inspected and appears circumferentially intact with no visible bowel leak. A drain is inserted, and hemostasis is confirmed.
